ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific egg fried rice or vegetarian egg fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried rice as a category. No specific vegetarian egg fried rice code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for egg fried rice or vegetarian fried rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of egg fried rice as served in a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists egg fried rice (plain, hawker-style). The vegetarian variant omits meat/seafood but retains egg, oil, and rice base. Values are adapted from the HPB egg fried rice entry; macros are consistent with USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice and whole egg composition cross-referenced for validation. Per-100g values reflect the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian egg fried rice. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for plain white rice in the range 64–72 and for mixed fried rice dishes in the range 48–56. Egg fried rice (hawker-style, white rice base with egg and oil) is estimated at GI 54, consistent with the moderating effect of fat and protein from egg on glycemic response. This is the closest defensible analogous estimate from an approved GI source. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 76.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 41.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 930 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full plate can contribute approximately 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from white rice base; estimated GI 54 (low) but glycemic load per standard serving is high at approximately 41
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150 g or substitute with brown rice where available, as the high glycemic load of a full plate may cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation despite the moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from egg and cooking oil; cholesterol approximately 82 mg per 100 g; elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume egg fried rice in moderation, preferring smaller portions and requesting less oil during preparation, as the combined saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium content may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian egg fried rice (煎蛋炒饭) is a classic Chinese-origin dish widely served across Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and restaurants. It is prepared by wok-frying pre-cooked white rice at high heat with beaten or pan-fried egg, vegetable oil, spring onion, soy sauce, salt, and white pepper. The high-heat wok technique produces the characteristic 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) aroma and a lightly smoky, savoury flavour. The texture is fluffy yet slightly chewy, with individual grains of rice separated and lightly coated in egg and oil. This vegetarian version omits meat and seafood, making it suitable for vegetarians and halal diners. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 489 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 76.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is energy-dense and carbohydrate-dominant due to the white rice base. Sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 930 mg per serving, primarily from soy sauce. The egg contributes dietary cholesterol, B vitamins, and fat-soluble micronutrients. Clinically, the dish carries a moderate glycemic load and moderate sodium burden, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is a common lunch and dinner staple in Singapore and is frequently ordered as a standalone meal or as a side dish accompanying other hawker dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg fried rice?
What is the GI of fried rice?
Can diabetics eat egg fried rice?
How much sodium is in egg fried rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo egg fried rice entry and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked white rice and whole egg. No exact match for vegetarian egg fried rice exists in HPB Foodo; the adaptation is defensible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fried rice category. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific vegetarian variant.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) consistent with the absence of hydrogenated fats in standard hawker-style preparation. GI is estimated at 54 (low) based on the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold of GI ≤ 55. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (54 × 76.5) / 100 = 41.3. The dish is classified as vegetarian (contains egg) but not vegan. Halal status is assumed based on standard vegetarian egg fried rice preparation without pork or alcohol-based sauces; individual restaurant practices may vary. The food_id GD003495 is preserved from the input seed record.

improvement_actions_taken: Cleaned food_name from menu-style 'R5. Egg Fried Rice (Vegetarian) 煎蛋炒饭' to canonical English 'Egg Fried Rice (Vegetarian)'.
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean multilingual requirement.
Populated per_100g nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo adapted for vegetarian egg fried rice, cross-validated with USDA SR Legacy.
Assigned GI 54 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice category.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Added health context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Set ontology match types to allowed values only: closest_parent and none.
